4 min read
Just now

--

Why frontend teams keep getting burned by supply chain attacks and the exact CI controls that stop them cold.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Last week I spent 4 hours auditing a client’s CI pipeline after their security team flagged a suspicious postinstall script. Turned out a transitive dependency had been quietly updated with credential-harvesting code. Nobody noticed. The lockfile was committed, but nobody was checking it meant anything.
This is not an edge case anymore.
The repeatable pattern I see
The npm supply chain attack is not new, but its scale and sophistication have escalated sharply. Phishing campaigns industrialized maintainer compromise, and self-propagating worms turned isolated incidents into chain reactions.
In 2026 alone, I keep seeing the same 4 symptoms in the frontend codebases I audit:

• A lockfile that’s committed but not enforced. npm install with no --frozen-lockfile flag will silently upgrade patch versions on a clean CI machine.

• Postinstall scripts running unchecked. Every npm install is a trust exercise in whatever code the maintainer shipped that day.

• No quarantine window on new releases. Your pipeline pulls packages the same day they are published. Zero time…
